examples.title: Basic Exit Status Check
examples.code:
#!/bin/bash
# Check if file exists and handle appropriately

if [ -f "$1" ]; then
 echo "File exists"
 exit 0 # Success
else
 echo "File does not exist" >&2
 exit 1 # General error
fi
examples.explanation: Simple script that checks file existence. Returns 0 if file found, 1 if not. Uses stderr for error message.

examples.title: EXIT Trap for Cleanup
examples.description: Using EXIT trap to ensure cleanup runs on any exit
examples.code:
#!/bin/bash
# Create temp file and ensure cleanup
TEMP_FILE="/tmp/script_temp_$$

# Cleanup function
cleanup() {
 echo "Cleaning up temp file..."
 rm -f "$TEMP_FILE"
}

# Register EXIT trap
trap cleanup EXIT

# Main script logic
echo "Creating temp file..."
touch "$TEMP_FILE"
echo "Processing..."
# Script will cleanup automatically on exit
examples.explanation: Demonstrates EXIT trap usage. cleanup() runs regardless of how script exits (success, error, or Ctrl+C).

examples.title: Pipeline with pipefail
examples.code:
#!/bin/bash
# Without pipefail - only checks last command
set +o pipefail # Default
grep "pattern" file.txt | sort | uniq
echo "Exit status: $?" # Only shows uniq's status

# With pipefail - catches any failure
set -o pipefail
grep "pattern" file.txt | sort | uniq
echo "Exit status: $?" # Shows first command that failed
examples.explanation: Demonstrates difference between default pipeline behavior and pipefail. Critical for detecting errors early in pipelines.

examples.title: Signal Handling and Exit Codes
examples.description: Handling signals and their exit codes
examples.code:
#!/bin/bash
# Script demonstrating signal handling

# Function to handle SIGINT (Ctrl+C)
handle_interrupt() {
 echo "\nScript interrupted! Exiting cleanly..."
 exit 130 # 128 + 2 (SIGINT)
}

# Register signal handlers
trap handle_interrupt SIGINT
trap 'echo "Received TERM"; exit 143' SIGTERM # 128+15

# Main loop
echo "Running... Press Ctrl+C to test"
while true; do
 sleep 1
done
examples.explanation: Demonstrates custom signal handling. Exit codes 130 and 143 clearly indicate which signal terminated the script.

examples.title: Function Return vs Exit
examples.code:
#!/bin/bash

# Function that returns status
check_file() {
 if [ -f "$1" ]; then
 return 0 # Success - only exits function
 else
 return 1 # Failure - only exits function
 fi
}

# Using the function
check_file "myfile.txt"
if [ $? -eq 0 ]; then
 echo "File found in function"
fi

# Exit that terminates entire script
test "$1" = "--quit" && exit 0 # Quits entire script
echo "This line runs unless --quit was passed"
examples.explanation: Shows that return only exits the function and returns control to caller, while exit terminates the entire script.

examples.title: Proper Error Propagation
examples.description: Propagating exit codes to parent processes
examples.code:
#!/bin/bash

# Function to perform operation with error handling
perform_operation() {
 # Validate input
 if [ -z "$1" ]; then
 echo "Usage: $0 <filename>" >&2
 return 2 # Return error code for usage
 fi
 
 # Try operation
 if ! command_that_may_fail "$1"; then
 return 1 # Return error code for failure
 fi
 
 return 0 # Success
}

# Call function and propagate exit status
perform_operation "$1"
exit $? # Exit with same status as function
examples.explanation: Demonstrates proper error propagation: function returns status, script uses 'exit $?' to pass that to parent.
